aliases: Indian prawn curry with fruits and nuts

english: Indian prawn curry with fruits and nuts

food_subgroup: Prawn and shrimp dishes — spiced curry with fruits and nuts

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for Kashmiri-style prawn curry specifically; FOODON_03302051 (shrimp dish) used as closest parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CJW (Crustaceans, cooked) is the closest defensible parent; no specific code for Kashmiri prawn curry exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Kashmiri prawn curry or sufficiently close prepared seafood curry dish.

main_ingredients: Kashmiri spices (cardamom, cinnamon, cloves, fennel, ginger)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Jhinga Kashmiri is approximately 250 g, comprising 6–8 medium-to-large prawns in a rich Kashmiri-spiced sauce with dried fruits and nuts.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Prawn curry / Shrimp curry (cooked, with spices and sauce); supplemented with ICMR-NIN data for dried fruits, nuts, and ghee components
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods provides values for prawn curry (cooked). Jhinga Kashmiri is a richer variant with added dried fruits (raisins, apricots) and nuts (cashews, almonds), which increases energy, fat, sugar, and carbohydrate relative to a plain prawn curry. Values are adapted upward from the ICMR-NIN prawn curry baseline to account for these additions, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cashews, raisins, and almonds to estimate the composite nutrient profile per 100 g of the finished dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — seafood curry / mixed seafood dish with sauce (analogous estimate); Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Jhinga Kashmiri. GI estimated at 40 based on analogous mixed seafood curry dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283. Prawns have negligible glycemic contribution; the primary glycemic load comes from dried fruits (raisins, apricots) and small amounts of onion and tomato in the sauce. The low-GI estimate (40) reflects the dominant protein and fat matrix of the dish, which attenuates glycemic response. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (40 × 7.8 × 2.5) / 100 = 7.8 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; ~1050 mg per 250 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150–180 g and request reduced-salt preparation, as a full restaurant serving can contribute approximately 44% of the 2400 mg daily sodium target.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~40) and moderate carbohydrate content (~7.8 g per 100 g), with carbohydrate primarily from dried fruits
type2_diabetes.guidance: Jhinga Kashmiri has a low estimated GI of approximately 40 and a moderate glycemic load of ~7.8 per serving, making it a suitable protein-rich option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice or naan.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol from prawns (~115 mg per 100 g) and saturated fat from ghee/yogurt (~2.8 g per 100 g), partially offset by beneficial omega-3 fatty acids in prawns and monounsaturated fats from nuts
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate portion size due to the cholesterol content of prawns and saturated fat from ghee; however, the presence of nuts and the omega-3 profile of prawns provide cardioprotective benefits that partially offset these concerns.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Jhinga Kashmiri is a traditional North Indian prawn curry originating from the Kashmir Valley, distinguished from other Indian prawn preparations by the inclusion of dried fruits such as raisins and apricots, and nuts such as cashews and almonds, which lend the dish a characteristic sweet-savoury flavour profile. The prawns are cooked in a rich, aromatic sauce made with onion, tomato, yogurt, ghee or oil, and a blend of Kashmiri spices including cardamom, cinnamon, cloves, fennel seed, and ginger, often finished with saffron for colour and fragrance. The texture is tender and succulent, with a sauce that is moderately thick, mildly spiced by Indian standards, and subtly sweet from the dried fruits. Nutritionally, Jhinga Kashmiri is a high-protein, moderate-fat dish providing approximately 148 kcal per 100 g, with 11.5 g protein and 8.2 g fat. The sodium content is elevated (~420 mg per 100 g) due to spice blends and cooking salt, making portion control relevant for individuals with hypertension. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 40 (low), making it appropriate for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes when served without high-GI staples. Prawns are a source of vitamin B12, selenium, phosphorus, and cholesterol; the nuts contribute vitamin E, magnesium, and monounsaturated fats. This dish is a culturally significant representation of Kashmiri Wazwan cuisine and is served in Indian restaurants across Singapore and internationally.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Kashmiri prawn curry?
Is Kashmiri prawn curry good for diabetics?
What nuts are in Jhinga Kashmiri?
Is Jhinga Kashmiri high in sodium?
What is the difference between Jhinga Kashmiri and regular prawn curry?
What spices are used in Jhinga Kashmiri?
meal_context: paired with naan or basmati rice

confidence_reason: No exact ICMR-NIN entry exists for Jhinga Kashmiri as a named dish. Nutrient values are adapted from the ICMR-NIN prawn curry baseline with upward adjustment for the dried fruit and nut components, cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy data for those ingredients. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for prawn curry or its components. All other -1 values in per_100g have been replaced with estimated numeric values derived from ICMR-NIN and USDA SR Legacy component data. Nutrient values represent an estimated composite for the finished dish at approximately 250 g restaurant serving size, normalised to per-100 g basis. The dish is served at Indian House / Dumpukht Grills & Curry, Boat Quay, Singapore (field survey listing). GI of 40 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Jhinga Kashmiri was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the accessed date. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_100g × serving_size_g) / 100 = (40 × 7.8 × 2.5) / 100 = 7.8 g.
